title: Employment Rights Act 1996 (Section 1 Statement) — as amended by the Employment Rights (Employment Particulars and Paid Annual Leave) (Amendment) Regulations 2018 and the Employment Rights (Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations 2019
chapters: Two implementing instruments: (1) SI 2018/1378 — Employment Rights (Employment Particulars and Paid Annual Leave) (Amendment) Regulations 2018, and (2) SI 2019/731 — Employment Rights (Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations 2019. Together they transform the ERA 1996 s.1 written statement of employment particulars from a document that could be provided within 2 months of starting work (to employees only) into a DAY-ONE RIGHT that must be provided on or before the first day of work to BOTH employees AND workers, with significantly expanded content requirements.

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Statutory Instruments — implementing the Good Work Plan (Government response to the Taylor Review of Modern Working Practices, 2018)
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Made by the Secretary of State for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy under the ERA 1996 s.7B and related powers
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Both instruments came into force on 6 April 2020. They apply to employees and workers whose employment/engagement starts ON OR AFTER 6 April 2020. Existing employees (those who started before 6 April 2020) are NOT automatically entitled to an updated statement — but if they REQUEST one, the employer must provide it within 1 MONTH. The expanded requirements also apply whenever there is a 'trigger' change in terms for existing employees.

precedingDocuments.title: Taylor Review of Modern Working Practices (July 2017)
precedingDocuments.reference: Good Work: the Taylor Review of Modern Working Practices
precedingDocuments.significance: The independent review by Matthew Taylor recommended that the right to a written statement should be extended to all workers (not just employees), should become a Day-One right (not within 2 months), and should include additional information to help workers understand their full entitlements. The Government accepted these recommendations in the 'Good Work Plan' (December 2018).

precedingDocuments.title: Good Work Plan (December 2018)
precedingDocuments.significance: The Government's response to the Taylor Review, committing to: extending the written statement to workers, making it a Day-One right, and expanding the required content. The 2018 and 2019 Regulations implement these commitments.

precedingDocuments.title: ERA 1996 s.1-7B (original written statement provisions)
precedingDocuments.significance: The original framework: employers had to provide a written statement to EMPLOYEES (not workers) within 2 MONTHS of starting work. The statement had to include specified particulars (name, date of employment, pay, hours, holiday, notice, etc.) but did not require information about probation, paid leave beyond holiday, benefits, or training.

leadingCases.name: No leading higher court case law on the 2020 reforms specifically
leadingCases.principle: The s.1 statement reforms have produced limited case law because the enforcement mechanism is weak: an employee/worker cannot bring a STANDALONE tribunal claim for failure to provide a compliant statement. They can only seek an additional award of 2-4 weeks' pay where they bring a SEPARATE successful claim (unfair dismissal, discrimination, wage deduction, etc.) and the employer is also in breach of s.1. However, the practical consequence of a non-compliant statement is significant: in disputes about terms (pay, hours, benefits, notice), the absence of a clear written record shifts the evidential advantage to the employee.
leadingCases.significance: SURVEILLANCE: monitor for tribunal decisions on the scope of the additional particulars required from April 2020, particularly 'benefits' and 'training' — which have caused drafting difficulties for employers.

parts.title: Part 1 — What Must Be Included: The Complete Checklist

sections.title: The 17 required particulars — what must be in the principal statement and what can be provided separately
sections.summary: The written statement must contain a comprehensive set of particulars covering the essential terms of the employment relationship. Since April 2020, the required content is significantly expanded. The particulars are divided into THREE CATEGORIES: (A) those that must be included in the PRINCIPAL STATEMENT (a single document provided on or before the first day), (B) those that may be provided in a REASONABLY ACCESSIBLE DOCUMENT (such as a staff handbook or intranet) referenced in the principal statement, and (C) those that may be provided in INSTALMENTS within 2 months of the start date.
sections.text:
CATEGORY A — MUST BE IN THE PRINCIPAL STATEMENT (single document, on or before Day 1):

(1) The employer's name.
(2) The employee's/worker's name.
(3) The date employment/engagement began.
(4) The date continuous employment began (if different — e.g., where service with a previous employer counts).
(5) The scale or rate of pay, or the method of calculating it.
(6) The intervals at which pay is made (weekly, monthly, etc.).
(7) Hours of work — including any terms about normal working hours and whether/how they may vary.
(8) WORKING PATTERN — the days of the week the employee/worker is required to work, and whether/how days may vary (NEW from April 2020).
(9) Holiday entitlement — including public holidays and holiday pay, sufficient to calculate the precise entitlement and any accrued holiday pay on termination.
(10) The place of work — or, if the employee works in various places, an indication of that and the employer's address.
(11) Job title or a brief description of the work.
(12) If not permanent: the expected duration or end date of fixed-term/temporary work.
(13) PROBATIONARY PERIOD — any probationary period, including its duration and any conditions (NEW from April 2020).
(14) BENEFITS — any benefits provided by the employer not covered elsewhere in the statement (NEW from April 2020).
(15) TRAINING — any training entitlement provided by the employer, and any MANDATORY training the employee/worker must complete, including whether the employer will pay for it (NEW from April 2020).

CATEGORY B — MAY BE IN A REASONABLY ACCESSIBLE DOCUMENT:

(16) Sick pay and incapacity terms — or reference to a document.
(17) OTHER PAID LEAVE — any paid leave entitlement beyond annual leave, including maternity, paternity, adoption, shared parental, parental bereavement (NEW from April 2020).
(18) Notice periods — the length of notice the employee must give and is entitled to receive.
(19) Pension schemes — whether a pension scheme exists and if so, whether the employer's contracting-out certificate is in force.

CATEGORY C — MAY BE PROVIDED IN INSTALMENTS WITHIN 2 MONTHS:

(20) Collective agreements — any collective agreements affecting the terms.
(21) Training entitlement (non-mandatory) — any training provided by the employer that is NOT compulsory.
(22) Disciplinary and grievance procedures — the applicable procedures, the identity of the person to whom the employee can appeal, and the person to whom a grievance should be addressed.

NOTE ON NEGATIVE STATEMENTS:
If there are NO particulars for a given heading (e.g., no pension scheme, no collective agreements, no probationary period), the statement must EXPLICITLY SAY SO — 'there are no applicable particulars'. Silence is not compliance.

WHO IS ENTITLED:
— EMPLOYEES: all employees, from Day One, regardless of length of engagement.
— WORKERS: all workers (including agency workers with a contract with the agency), from Day One.
— The previous exclusion for engagements lasting less than one month has been REMOVED.

TIMING:
— New starters from 6 April 2020: principal statement on or before Day 1. Category C items within 2 months.
— Existing employees (started before 6 April 2020): NOT automatically entitled to an updated statement. BUT: if they REQUEST one, the employer must provide it within 1 MONTH. Also, any 'trigger' change in terms after 6 April 2020 requires an updated statement to be provided 'at the earliest opportunity and not later than 1 month after the change'.

ENFORCEMENT:
— An employee/worker CANNOT bring a standalone tribunal claim for failure to provide a compliant s.1 statement.
— BUT: if the employee/worker brings a SEPARATE successful claim (unfair dismissal, discrimination, wage deduction, breach of contract), AND the employer is in breach of s.1, the tribunal MUST award an additional 2 WEEKS' PAY and MAY award up to 4 WEEKS' PAY.
— In practice, the real risk is EVIDENTIAL: where terms are disputed and no written statement exists, the tribunal is likely to prefer the employee's version of the terms.

CRITICAL DISTINCTION — THE WRITTEN STATEMENT IS NOT THE CONTRACT:

The s.1 written statement is EVIDENCE of the terms of the employment contract — it is NOT the contract itself. The employment contract is the ACTUAL AGREEMENT between the parties, which may include:
(a) Express terms — what was explicitly agreed (in writing, verbally, or by conduct).
(b) Implied terms — terms implied by statute (e.g., ERA s.86 notice, EqA 2010 equality clause), by common law (e.g., mutual trust and confidence, duty to provide a safe system of work), or by custom and practice.
(c) Terms incorporated from collective agreements — where a recognised trade union has negotiated terms that are incorporated into individual contracts.
(d) Terms from staff handbooks — where the handbook is contractual (or specific provisions within it are).

If the written statement CONTRADICTS what was actually agreed (e.g., the statement says 25 days' holiday but 28 was agreed verbally at interview), the ACTUAL AGREEMENT prevails — but the written statement is strong evidence in any dispute. This is why accuracy matters: an employer who issues a statement containing terms they did not intend to offer may find those terms are binding.

Conversely, an employer who provides NO statement is at a permanent evidential disadvantage — in any dispute about terms, the tribunal will determine what the terms ought to have been (ERA ss.11-12), considering: what was said at interview, the job advertisement, comparable employees' terms, industry norms, and custom and practice. The employer who cannot produce a written record of what was agreed will typically lose these arguments.

For the HR manager, the practical consequence is clear: the written statement should accurately reflect the ACTUAL agreement, be issued on or before Day One, and be updated whenever terms change. It is both a legal obligation and the employer's best protection in any future dispute.
sections.keyPrinciple: The written statement is the FOUNDATION of the employment relationship. Every dispute about terms — pay, hours, benefits, notice, probation — starts with the question 'what does the contract say?' If the employer has not provided a compliant written statement, the employer is at a PERMANENT evidential disadvantage. The £643 penalty (2-4 weeks' pay) is modest — but the real cost is losing every future argument about what was agreed. The HR manager must ensure that EVERY new starter from Day One receives a compliant principal statement. Templates must be reviewed and updated to include the April 2020 additions: working pattern, probation, benefits, training, and other paid leave.
refs: ERA 1996 ss.1-7B (written statement provisions — as amended)
SI 2018/1378 (Employment Rights (Employment Particulars and Paid Annual Leave) (Amendment) Regulations 2018)
Taylor Review of Modern Working Practices (July 2017)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Employment contracts and conditions
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the written statement requirements, including a template and checklist.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Written statement of employment particulars
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on what must be included, with practical examples and FAQs.

commonErrors: Not providing the statement on Day One — since April 2020, the principal statement must be provided ON OR BEFORE the first day of work. Providing it within 2 months is no longer compliant for the principal statement (though some Category C items can still be provided within 2 months)

Not extending the statement to WORKERS — since April 2020, workers (not just employees) are entitled to a written statement. This includes casual workers, zero-hours workers, and agency workers with a contract with the agency. Many employers still only provide statements to employees

Not including the NEW particulars added from April 2020 — working pattern, probationary period, benefits, mandatory training, and other paid leave. Templates created before April 2020 that have not been updated are NON-COMPLIANT

Using silence instead of negative statements — if there is no pension scheme, no probationary period, or no collective agreement, the statement must EXPLICITLY SAY SO. Leaving the field blank or omitting the heading is non-compliant

Including discretionary benefits in the statement without careful drafting — the requirement to list 'benefits' risks converting discretionary benefits (such as an enhanced maternity pay scheme or a discretionary bonus) into CONTRACTUAL entitlements. The statement should clearly distinguish between contractual and discretionary benefits

Not providing an updated statement when existing employees request one — existing employees (pre-April 2020) can request an updated statement. The employer must provide it within 1 MONTH. Failing to respond is a breach

Not updating the statement when terms change — ANY change to the particulars must be communicated in writing 'at the earliest opportunity and not later than 1 month after the change'. Verbal notification is insufficient

Treating the written statement as 'just an HR form' rather than a LEGAL DOCUMENT — the written statement defines the employer's obligations and the employee's entitlements. It is the primary evidence in any dispute about terms. Getting it wrong has consequences far beyond the 2-4 weeks' pay penalty

Confusing the written statement with the employment contract — the statement is EVIDENCE of the terms, not the contract itself. The contract includes express, implied, and incorporated terms that may not appear in the statement. But the statement is powerful evidence: if terms are disputed and the statement says X, the employer will need strong evidence to prove that Y was actually agreed

versions.text: Original ERA 1996 s.1: written statement to EMPLOYEES only, within 2 MONTHS. Excluded engagements under 1 month. Limited required content.
versions.policyRationale: The original framework balanced the need for documentation with the administrative burden on employers. The 2-month window and employee-only scope reflected a less protective approach.

versions.text: CURRENT: Day-One right for EMPLOYEES AND WORKERS. Expanded content: working pattern, probation, benefits, training, other paid leave. No exclusion for short engagements.
versions.amendedBy: SI 2018/1378 + SI 2019/731 (implementing the Good Work Plan)
versions.policyRationale: The Taylor Review and Good Work Plan recognised that workers — particularly those in the gig economy and non-standard employment — needed clear documentation of their terms from the outset. Extending the right to workers and making it a Day-One requirement addressed the information asymmetry that left vulnerable workers without knowledge of their entitlements.

pendingAmendments.instrument: ERA 2025 — potential further expansion of written statement requirements
pendingAmendments.status: The ERA 2025 may introduce further requirements for written statements, including information about trade union rights and the right to request flexible working. Monitor secondary legislation.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — any expansion of required content will require template updates
